Q1: I organized my study routine around intensive blocks rather than spreading things out evenly. I'd dedicate about 30 hours a week, but honestly, I'd batch it into longer sessions when I felt focused. Being a visual learner, I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps from the course materials. I found that once I got into a flow state, I could cover a lot of ground, so I'd protect those periods fiercely.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was coming up. I'd have weeks where I was super consistent, logging in daily and watching lectures, then other weeks where I'd barely touch the LMS because I was deep into synthesizing everything offline. Deadlines would trigger more intense bursts of activity, but I tried to stay ahead enough that it wasn't pure panic mode. The stress was always there though, even when I was on top of things.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check for new announcements or to access video lectures for the seven courses I was taking. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if discussion threads had new responses to things I'd posted. There was also this habit of checking my grades after submissions came back, which honestly added to my stress even when I did well. The LMS became like a hub I'd orbit around constantly.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. I'd login during breakfast, scroll through updates, and mentally plan my day around what needed attention. It happened naturally because with seven online courses, if I didn't stay on top of notifications, I'd miss something important. The routine felt necessary for survival more than anything else.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm such a visual learner. I watched over 100 minutes of content and often rewatched complex sections. The discussion forums were surprisingly useful too because explaining concepts to peers helped solidify my understanding. I also appreciated any visual aids, diagrams, or infographics the instructors provided because they matched how my brain processes information.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't always complete every reading assignment, which probably explains my 51% assignment completion rate. Long text-heavy PDFs without visual elements felt like a slog, and I'd often skim them or skip to find the key points. Some supplementary materials felt redundant if the video already covered the concept well. I had to triage given my course load, so less engaging resources got deprioritized.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessment outcomes or genuinely deepened my understanding. Visual content, interactive elements, or discussions that challenged my thinking all passed that test. I also valued anything that helped me see the bigger picture of how concepts connected. If something felt like busywork or repetitive, I'd mentally check out even if I technically completed it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford with seven courses demanding attention. Required activities had this weight to them, this pressure that made me engage even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff I'd only do if I was genuinely curious or if I had unexpected free time, which was rare. The stress of keeping up meant optional often became synonymous with skippable in my mind.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and annotate them on my tablet, creating visual summaries that I'd review repeatedly. I also used external YouTube videos and Khan Academy to supplement topics that weren't clicking. My physical notebook was full of hand-drawn concept maps that represented hours of work the LMS would never capture.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in review mode, working from printed materials and my own notes without touching the LMS once. I'd sometimes have study sessions with friends where we'd work through problems together using a whiteboard. The CBT sessions I attended also helped me process course stress, but that mental work of managing my anxiety and building focus strategies wouldn't show up anywhere in the data.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video lecture once online, download the slides, and then spend hours working with that material offline. My first pass through content was usually on the LMS, but the real learning happened when I was reorganizing information into visual formats on my own. The LMS would show one view, but I might have spent triple that time studying the same material elsewhere.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple courses had overlapping deadlines. My attendance dropped to around 22% for live sessions because I was overwhelmed trying to catch up on recorded content instead. The high stress I carry made it hard to focus sometimes, and my low resilience meant setbacks hit me harder than they probably should have. Those weeks felt like treading water.
Q13: The workload from seven courses absolutely affected my LMS usage. When I was fatigued, I'd log in but just passively scroll without really absorbing anything. Confusion in one course would make me avoid it temporarily while I focused on subjects where I felt more confident. Competing responsibilities meant I had to constantly make strategic choices about where to invest my limited energy and attention.
Q14: Yes, definitely. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, stressed about an upcoming exam or just mentally exhausted. I'd technically log the watch time, but I wasn't really present. Other times I'd click through discussion posts without genuinely engaging with the ideas. It was like going through the motions to feel productive without actually being productive.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my completion rate is around 52%. Even knowing something was important, if it felt overwhelming or if I wasn't sure how to approach it, I'd push it back. My low resilience meant that facing difficult tasks felt extra daunting. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt fresher, but tomorrow often brought its own challenges.
Q16: Deadlines were like gravity wells that pulled all my attention and energy toward them. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd suddenly become hyper-focused, logging in multiple times a day, watching videos at 1.5x speed, and frantically posting in discussions. It wasn't ideal, but the pressure of deadlines was often what finally converted my preparation into actual submissions.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was positive, which reinforced that my visual study methods were working. Getting good scores on exams gave me confidence to keep pushing through the stress. However, critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that course temporarily. The CBT sessions helped me reframe feedback as useful rather than threatening, which improved how I responded over time.
Q18: The course structure heavily influenced when and how I participated. Courses with clear weekly modules and visual roadmaps were easier for me to navigate and stay engaged with. When content was organized logically with good visual hierarchy, I felt more in control. Courses that felt disorganized or text-heavy made me anxious and more likely to fall behind or disengage.
Q19: Visual elements in course design made engagement much easier for me. Clear timelines, progress indicators, and well-organized modules reduced my cognitive load significantly. What made things harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when navigation was confusing. Having seven courses meant I had no patience for poor design; I'd just find workarounds or external resources instead.
Q20: I think that pattern could reflect students who are exploring and trying to understand material but struggling to synthesize it into completed work. Maybe they're perfectionists who keep revising but never feel ready to submit. It could also be anxiety-related, where engaging with content feels safer than the vulnerability of submitting work for judgment. High activity might mask avoidance of the final, scary step of turning something in.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was pretty low, but my exam scores were strong because I was doing deep learning offline. Students who understand how they learn best might not need constant LMS interaction to succeed. They might download materials early, study independently, and only return to submit work. The LMS activity is just the tip of the iceberg for how learning actually happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and completion of specific tasks. They're good at capturing frequency and duration of interactions. But they completely miss the quality of attention during those interactions, the offline studying that might be more substantial, and the emotional and psychological dimensions of learning. My stress levels and the work I do managing them are invisible to any log.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with ideas, not just passively consuming content. It's when I'm making connections between concepts, creating my own visual representations, and genuinely caring about understanding deeply. True engagement for me involves emotional investment and curiosity, not just time spent. It's the difference between watching a video and actually thinking about what it means for my understanding.
Q24: The LMS would never show the hours I spent in CBT sessions learning to manage my academic stress and anxiety. It wouldn't capture the elaborate visual study systems I created offline or the peer study sessions that happened in person. My internal experience of being surprised by how much I could achieve despite my struggles, or the constant background stress I carry, those are invisible. The data shows what I did, not who I am as a learner.
